note: Ladder rung 1: TTL (CacheTTL/Continuum, arXiv 2511.02230), the first non-LRU policy measured. The first partial answer to the project's question (how much of LRU's 60% over-oracle do heuristics collect): this one collects NEGATIVE room.
finding: Fixed-TTL is dominated by LRU at every setting and converges to LRU only as TTL -> infinity (proactive expiry never fires). Mechanism: in append-only agentic conversations an idle block is typically re-accessed on the next turn (the whole prefix is re-read), so idle-time expiry systematically evicts blocks that will be needed. Idle != dead, the same truth the semantic-ephemeral analysis surfaced. A naive lifecycle heuristic can HURT on this workload; this is exactly the wins-and-losses honesty the benchmark exists to report.
fidelity_caveat: This is the FIXED-TTL variant. The paper derives TTL adaptively from reload cost and queueing delay and pins KV specifically across tool-call gaps; that adaptive, gap-aware version needs the live serving loop the offline simulator does not model, and could perform materially better. Recorded in the policy docstring so the authors see exactly what differs. The negative result is about fixed-TTL, not a claim about CacheTTL's adaptive design.
